Brandon? I just had a quick question actually regarding... Quick now, remember. A quick one. A quick one. Yeah. Regarding the relationship stuff you talked about earlier, where you want to give enough that you're building a relationship with the customer but at the same time you don't want to give too much that it goes over the edge. You talk a lot about yourself and your autobiography and you know, Carly used to be in your business and you talk a lot also, you show Scott Tucker's work where he always includes his dog and that becomes part of his stick so where do you draw the line? Well, when you got Tucker's personality you need like a dog, everybody's gonna like. I mean, you know, that's like essential. I mean, you want to show Charlie Manson with rabbits or something. Little bunnies, look at my little bunny. It takes the edge off of having axe murdered 20 something people. There's a fairly long answer to your question which is actually in tomorrow's material. The short answer to your question is you reveal personal things strategically like anything else. So what you can't judge by looking at what I do is what personal things I choose not to reveal. You can only see the ones obviously that I choose to reveal. There's careful thought about all of that. None of it is accidental. But generally speaking what it does is it allows people to identify with you in different ways. So for example, the revelation of having been bankrupt always worked for me and always works for me. Why? In any audience of entrepreneurs, 100 or more, there's somebody in there that's been bankrupt. And there's another two or three that have been on the edge. So, and they think it's a deep dark secret. And for the most part they've kept it to themselves and in some cases they're embarrassed about it. And in some cases they think it's some kind of detriment to success and so forth. So literally as soon as I talk about it, I get them. It wouldn't matter what else I was doing, I got them. In every audience there's either a stutterer or a parent of a stutterer. As soon as I talk about it, I get them. It doesn't matter what I'm selling, doesn't matter who they are, I got them. So it's sort of knocking off numbers, small numbers multiplied. Same thing happens if they read it. So most of that stuff is like in the no harm, no file category, but it's going to resonate with certain people and it's going to attract individuals that it wouldn't have attracted otherwise. It also has the virtue of not having things hidden and the fewer of them the better. Because now I never have to worry about somebody deciding that the bankruptcy was a big deal. Well I've told everybody about it. So on that level it's like I can get him with this, I can get him with this, I can get him with this, and boom that gets me another 18 responses. On another level it's about strengthening the human part of the bond, again because it'll only be with you for a certain period of time for the core stuff, right, then they're with you because of their relationship. And the tricky part is not to do it in such a way that it invites a different kind of relationship than you want. If I was doing, if we went down the list, I got the list, we'll look at it tomorrow, if I was doing most of what I have done in personal disclosure without counterbalancing it with some of the deliberate reputation for grumpy, tough, unpleasant, arbitrary, difficult, if I took that away I would create a lot of nightmares for myself with the other list. It's only because I do the one I'm actually able to do a lot of the other.
